title: Legacy Archive Strategy
use_for: preserving old implementation evidence during a rewrite
excluding archived code from compilation and runtime discovery
using old code as a requirement and anti-pattern source
preventing new code from depending on archived implementation
do_not_use_for: shipping legacy code as part of runtime
long-term support of two active implementations
behavior-preserving refactors where old code remains live
rules: archive old code instead of deleting it when requirements are still being extracted
exclude the archive from compile, packaging, runtime discovery, tests, and generated outputs unless explicitly inspected
never let new runtime code reference archived code
annotate archived evidence by intent and risk rather than fixing it in place
treat archive organization as documentation, not as architecture
procedure: Choose a clear archive boundary and verify the build system excludes it.
Move old implementation evidence into the archive without changing behavior inside the archived files.
Add classification notes outside or near archived groups when useful: preserve intent, redesign, delete behavior, investigate, or do not copy.
Search for compile includes, package includes, test discovery, generated code, reflection scans, and runtime registration that could still see the archive.
Define a rule that new code may cite archived intent in notes but may not import, reference, or instantiate archived implementation.
Periodically retire archived groups only after their product intent has been resolved.
output: no-reference rule for new runtime code

primary_when: the task is primarily about preserving old code outside the build during a rewrite
the task is about using old code as reference without keeping it active
must_not_own_when: the task is primarily about designing the new runtime architecture
the old implementation must remain active and supported

solves: how to avoid losing old product knowledge during a rewrite
how to keep archived implementation from contaminating the new runtime
how to turn old code into explicit requirements and anti-patterns

core_principles: Archived code answers what existed and what may need redesign; it is not a runtime dependency.
Build exclusion is part of the archive contract, not a convenience.
Legacy intent may be preserved while legacy structure is rejected.
The archive should reduce uncertainty, not become a second product.
constraints: Do not use project-specific archive paths in reusable guidance.
Do not compile archived implementation by default.
Do not patch archived code to satisfy new architecture.
Do not allow new code to depend on archive symbols, resources, or registration.
decision_rules: If old code is not yet understood, archive it and mark it investigate rather than deleting it.
If old code demonstrates a bad solution to a real requirement, mark preserve intent and do not copy.
If archived code is accidentally compiled, fix the build boundary before continuing the rewrite.
If new code needs something from the archive, extract the requirement and implement it cleanly in the new model.

preferred_patterns: classification notes for unresolved legacy evidence
one-way dependency from design notes to archive evidence
periodic archive retirement after requirement resolution
avoid_patterns: legacy code included through wildcard compile rules
new runtime wrappers around archived implementation
fixing archived code instead of rebuilding the capability
using archive folder structure as the new architecture

recipes.name: Archive old code for a rewrite
recipes.when: Use when old implementation should remain available as evidence but must not participate in the new build.
steps: Move old implementation into a clearly named archive boundary.
Update compile, package, test, and generation rules to exclude the archive.
Run a build or dry-run file list to prove the archive is invisible.
Classify archived groups by intent and risk.
Implement new code from extracted requirements, not archive references.

pitfalls: Assuming a folder move excludes code from wildcard compilation.
Letting tests or generators discover archived code.
Copying old architecture because it is still easy to browse.
Treating archive cleanup as more important than requirement extraction.
review_checklist: Is the archive excluded from compile and package outputs?
Are new runtime references to archive code impossible or forbidden?
Are archived capabilities classified by product intent?
Are anti-patterns documented as do not copy rather than silently remembered?
Can archived groups be retired when their intent has been resolved?

references.title: Stale Coupling Audit
references.interpretation: Use to find hidden links that may keep archived code active.
references.why: Archive boundaries fail when stale coupling remains in build, runtime, config, or generated paths.
